request.question: Kyle MacLachlan's role as Dale Cooper was featured on what network in 2017?

title: Twin Peaks (2017 TV series)

sentences: Twin Peaks, also known as Twin Peaks: The Return, is an American mystery drama television revival series created by Mark Frost and David Lynch.
It is a continuation of the 1990–91 ABC series of the same name.
The limited series consists of 18 episodes and premiered on Showtime on May 21, 2017, following a world premiere on May 19, 2017, at The Theatre at Ace Hotel.
The series was developed and written by Lynch and Frost over several years and directed by Lynch.
An ensemble of returning and new cast members appear, led by original star Kyle MacLachlan.

FBI Special Agent Dale Bartholomew Cooper, portrayed by Kyle MacLachlan, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the ABC television series "Twin Peaks" and Showtime's continuation of the same name.
He also plays a supporting role in the prequel film "".
Audrey Horne is a fictional character from the ABC television series "Twin Peaks", played by Sherilyn Fenn.
The character was created by David Lynch.
She was introduced in the pilot.

The daughter of Ben (Richard Beymer) and Sylvia Horne, sister of Johnny Horne (Robert Bauer) and half-sister of Donna Hayward (Lara Flynn Boyle), her storylines focused on her infatuation with the series protagonist Dale Cooper (Kyle MacLachlan), infiltrating the brothel/casino One Eyed Jacks and becoming an activist through civil disobedience.
Orson Hodge is a fictional character on the ABC television series "Desperate Housewives".
The character is played by Kyle MacLachlan.
Orson is introduced in the final episodes of the second season of the series, and becomes the main mystery of the third season.
MacLachlan left the main cast in season six, but made guest appearances until the eighth and final season.
"Episode 4", also known as "The One-Armed Man", is the fifth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".
The episode was written by Robert Engels, and directed by Tim Hunter.
"Episode 4" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Piper Laurie and Richard Beymer, and introduces series co-creator David Lynch in the role of Gordon Cole.
"Episode 5", also known as "Cooper's Dreams", is the sixth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".
The episode was written by series co-creator Mark Frost and directed by Lesli Linka Glatter.
"Episode 5" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Richard Beymer, with guest appearances by Chris Mulkey and David Patrick Kelly.
"Blue Velvet" is a 1986 American mystery film written and directed by David Lynch.
The movie exhibits elements of both film noir and surrealism.
The film features Kyle MacLachlan, Isabella Rossellini, Dennis Hopper, and Laura Dern.
The title is taken from the 1963 Bobby Vinton song of the same name, which is featured in the film.
Although initially detested by some mainstream critics, the film has now become widely acclaimed.
Kyle Merritt MacLachlan ( ; born February 22, 1959) is an American actor.

MacLachlan is best known for his role as Dale Cooper in "Twin Peaks" (1990–1991; 2017) and its film prequel "" (1992), as well as roles in two of David Lynch's films: Paul Atreides in "Dune" (1984) and Jeffrey Beaumont in "Blue Velvet" (1986).

MacLachlan's other film roles include Lloyd Gallagher in "The Hidden" (1987), Ray Manzarek in "The Doors" (1991), Clifford Vandercave in "The Flintstones" (1994), Zack Carey in "Showgirls" (1995), and the voice of Riley's dad in "Inside Out" (2015).
Valerie "Val" Cooper is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character works for the Office of National Emergency.
She is most notable for her work as the liaison for mutant affairs in the United States government.

She once claimed to have been inspired to government service by the interesting cases her brother, an FBI agent, encountered in his work (it was implied, but never stated outright, that her brother is Dale Cooper from "Twin Peaks").
The pilot episode, also known as Northwest Passage, of the mystery television series "Twin Peaks" premiered on the ABC Network on Sunday, April 8, 1990.
It was written by series creators Mark Frost and David Lynch, and directed by Lynch.

The pilot follows the characters of Dale Cooper and Harry S. Truman as they investigate the death of popular high school student Laura Palmer; Cooper believes the murder has connections to a murder case that occurred a year earlier.
In addition to setting the tone for the show, the episode sets up several character and story arcs and marked the appearance of several recurring characters.
The episode received a strong Nielsen household rating compared to other season one episodes and was well received by fans and critics alike.
The original title for the series was "Northwest Passage", but this was later changed.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Kyle MacLachlan's role as Dale Cooper was featured on what network in 2017?
hop1_docs: Kyle MacLachlan | Kyle Merritt MacLachlan ( ; born February 22, 1959) is an American actor.  MacLachlan is best known for his role as Dale Cooper in "Twin Peaks" (1990–1991; 2017) and its film prequel "" (1992), as well as roles in two of David Lynch's films: Paul Atreides in "Dune" (1984) and Jeffrey Beaumont in "Blue Velvet" (1986).  MacLachlan's other film roles include Lloyd Gallagher in "The Hidden" (1987), Ray Manzarek in "The Doors" (1991), Clifford Vandercave in "The Flintstones" (1994), Zack Carey in "Showgirls" (1995), and the voice of Riley's dad in "Inside Out" (2015).

Dale Cooper | FBI Special Agent Dale Bartholomew Cooper, portrayed by Kyle MacLachlan, is a fictional character and the protagonist of the ABC television series "Twin Peaks" and Showtime's continuation of the same name.  He also plays a supporting role in the prequel film "".

Audrey Horne | Audrey Horne is a fictional character from the ABC television series "Twin Peaks", played by Sherilyn Fenn.  The character was created by David Lynch.  She was introduced in the pilot.  The daughter of Ben (Richard Beymer) and Sylvia Horne, sister of Johnny Horne (Robert Bauer) and half-sister of Donna Hayward (Lara Flynn Boyle), her storylines focused on her infatuation with the series protagonist Dale Cooper (Kyle MacLachlan), infiltrating the brothel/casino One Eyed Jacks and becoming an activist through civil disobedience.

Episode 5 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 5", also known as "Cooper's Dreams", is the sixth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by series co-creator Mark Frost and directed by Lesli Linka Glatter.  "Episode 5" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Richard Beymer, with guest appearances by Chris Mulkey and David Patrick Kelly.

Episode 3 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 3", later also known as "Rest in Pain", is the fourth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by Harley Peyton, and directed by Tina Rathborne.  "Episode 3" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Michael Ontkean and Ray Wise, and introduces Sheryl Lee's second role, Maddy Ferguson.

Episode 4 (Twin Peaks) | "Episode 4", also known as "The One-Armed Man", is the fifth episode of the first season of the American mystery television series "Twin Peaks".  The episode was written by Robert Engels, and directed by Tim Hunter.  "Episode 4" features series regulars Kyle MacLachlan, Piper Laurie and Richard Beymer, and introduces series co-creator David Lynch in the role of Gordon Cole.

The Hidden (film) | The Hidden is an American science fiction action-horror film produced and released in 1987 by New Line Cinema.  The film was written by Bob Hunt (pen name of writer/producer/director Jim Kouf) and directed by Jack Sholder.  The cast features Kyle MacLachlan and Michael Nouri and includes supporting roles by Clu Gulager, Chris Mulkey, Ed O'Ross, Clarence Felder, Claudia Christian and Larry Cedar.  A sequel, "The Hidden II", directed by Seth Pinsker was released in 1993.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Kyle MacLachlan: Passage [1] — "role as Dale Cooper in 'Twin Peaks' (1990–1991; 2017)". 
  - Dale Cooper: Passage [2] — "protagonist of the ABC television series 'Twin Peaks' and Showtime's continuation".
- Ambiguities:
  - Two network candidates appear in the passages: "ABC" and "Showtime" (both from passage [2]). Passage [1] notes the 2017 year but does not state the network for 2017.
  - A dedicated 2017-series page/title (e.g., a "Twin Peaks (2017)" variant) is not present among the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as named in passages): "Twin Peaks".
  - Missing fact needed: the specific network that aired the 2017 Twin Peaks continuation (i.e., which network featured Kyle MacLachlan's Dale Cooper in 2017).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Twin Peaks" page — check the lede/infobox and sections like "Broadcast", "Release", or "Production" for where the 2017 series aired. (Search: "Twin Peaks 2017 network")
  2) Open the "Dale Cooper" page — check the lede and references for the phrase "Showtime's continuation" and any citation that identifies the 2017 broadcaster.
  3) Open the "Kyle MacLachlan" page — check the lede/filmography and reference notes for the 2017 Twin Peaks entry and its cited network.
  4) If present, open a specific 2017-series page or disambiguation (e.g., "Twin Peaks (2017)" or similar) and check its lede/infobox for network information.
Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and checked.
hop2_docs: Twin Peaks (2017 TV series) | Twin Peaks, also known as Twin Peaks: The Return, is an American mystery drama television revival series created by Mark Frost and David Lynch.  It is a continuation of the 1990–91 ABC series of the same name.  The limited series consists of 18 episodes and premiered on Showtime on May 21, 2017, following a world premiere on May 19, 2017, at The Theatre at Ace Hotel.  The series was developed and written by Lynch and Frost over several years and directed by Lynch.  An ensemble of returning and new cast members appear, led by original star Kyle MacLachlan.
Double R Diner | The Double R Diner is a fictional diner in the American television series "Twin Peaks" and the 2017 revival of "Twin Peaks".
List of Twin Peaks characters | The following is a list of characters from the television series "Twin Peaks", the film "", and the 2017 revival.

The Secret History of Twin Peaks | The Secret History of Twin Peaks is an epistolary (dossier-style) novel by Mark Frost, which provides background information on the history of the fictional town and characters from the television series "Twin Peaks".  The book was published in October 2016, several months before the debut of a new season of the series in May 2017.  A follow-up book, "Twin Peaks: The Final Dossier", also written by Frost, will be released after the new series has aired.

Laura Palmer | Laura Palmer ( ) is a fictional character in the "Twin Peaks" franchise.  She is portrayed by Sheryl Lee and was created by the series creators David Lynch and Mark Frost.  She first appears in the ABC original series "Twin Peaks".  A high school student whose death is the catalyst for the events of the series, Palmer is the protagonist in Lynch's prequel films "" (1992) and "" (2014), which both depict the final week of her life leading up to her murder.  Laura also appears in the novels, "Twin Peaks: The Return" (2017), and a variety of merchandise based on the series.

Music of Twin Peaks | The music of the American television series "Twin Peaks", its 1992 prequel film "", and its 2017 revival series was composed by Angelo Badalamenti.  "Twin Peaks"' co-creator David Lynch wrote lyrics for five songs used throughout the series—including "Falling", "The Nightingale", "Into the Night", "Just You" and "Sycamore Trees"—and three songs featured in "Twin Peaks: Fire Walk with Me", including "A Real Indication", "Questions in a World of Blue" and "The Black Dog Runs at Night".  Julee Cruise, who made cameo appearances in both the series and film, provided vocals for four of Lynch's and Badalamenti's collaborations, and jazz vocalist Jimmy Scott performed on "Sycamore Trees".  Three of the series' actors—James Marshall, Lara Flynn Boyle, and Sheryl Lee—provided vocals for "Just You".

Part 8 (Twin Peaks) | "Part 8", also known as "The Return, Part 8", is the eighth episode of "Twin Peaks"' revival series.  It was written by Mark Frost and David Lynch and directed by Lynch.  "Part 8" was broadcast on June 25, 2017, on Showtime and was watched by an audience of 246,000 viewers in the United States.  The episode was composed of lengthy, surreal scenes, with very little dialogue throughout.  It received critical acclaim.
hop2_titles: The Secret History of Twin Peaks
response.summary_2: Showtime. Twin Peaks (2017 TV series) premiered on Showtime on May 21, 2017, as a continuation of the 1990–91 ABC series.
